item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated and crosslinked to SCP-2747 as part of the Grand Wikiwalk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore this interpretation. It's what a few people suspected when they first read it. I really think it's heartbreaking and very deep when viewed this way.

Ultimately I think the truth lies somewhere in the middle. Not quite anafabula, not quite sorrow and regret, maybe the netherwhere between.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting…

+ My own 'what I took from it' interpretation
- Like I said above, 'How many holes before it all unravels?':

I read it as an O5 reminiscing on their career… what they remember of it, that is.

Amnestics are a fix-all to the Foundation. Exposed to a cogitohazard? Amnestics. Survive a 682 breach too traumatized to function? Amnestics. Reality warper goes berserk in Manhattan? Amestics for everyone. If no one remembers a thing, and you destroy the evidence, it never happened. Cut out the flaw in reality, patch over the hole, good as new… so long as no one looks at the seam too closely. How many holes can you cut and patch in reality before it all unravels?

Digging deeper, they use amnestics on themselves as often as on civilians. How much time does a typical Foundation employee loose over their career? How much trauma and guilt do they expunge from their own minds? How much do they choose to unsee? How many names do they choose to unlearn? How many holes can you cut in your own mind before it all unravels?

And for the O5…the guilt of decisions made for the greater good, the terror of seeing the world unfiltered by the lies you tell others so they can sleep, the burden of knowing that the end of the world is always just one mistake away (and that that mistake could be yours)… and the one great temptation of knowing there is a simple method to just make it all…go away. How many holes in your soul…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad at the positive reception this received!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the record, this is neither about Montauk nor 682. It uses them as literary device, but it's not about them. Nor is it a manner of guess the skip. This is its own thing.

+ As many don't understand, here's the explanation
- SPOLIERS

Nominally this poem is related to SCP-2747, but moreso it's an angry, surrealist abstraction of the SCP format. The object is a collection of dumb horror cliches, the subtextual emotion is at the forefront, the interview is nothing but quotes from other interviews, and the containment procedures no longer exist.

This shows the degrading of the Foundation as a narrative and as a concept. The anafabula was generated by imagery and iconography from all the skips mentioned as they are stories within our fictional universe. As the anafabula begins to eat the narrative of the Foundation, this is what will happen. Things start to blur and become unsure. We have no way of telling if this is a new SCP or one we've read before. Concepts start to repeat. Sentences become meaningless. Before the stanzas disappear entirely we are left desperately clinging to our concepts, but before we realize it, our stories are eaten and we fall into the void.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In general, I don't usually find poetry to be an appropriate medium for the wiki. This is not to say that it's impossible that I would support something like that ever, but I have yet to encounter an example of it that I like here. I find poetry to be far more intimate and emotionally relatable than most of the material that we work with here. It's that seriousness that makes poetry kind of cringey when it doesn't work well. I'll have more thoughts on this matter elsewhere.

This article isn't really changing my mind on that. Here, it's poetry as a "guess which skip it is" game more than anything else. I'm not really able to find a whole lot of emotional resonance in a giant murder lizard, and overall the subjects of the various stanzas are articles that have been covered so many times by now that I feel mostly boredom when I read anything about them. As an aside, I eagerly await the day when the site can put aside its obsession with Montauk, but I also realize that I'll be waiting a very long time.

Poetry has to carry a certain personal weight to it for it to be effective. And I'm not convinced the more well-worn, high profile monster articles and other mainstays are capable of being translated in such a manner as to impart that weight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, for me, the last stanza explains nothing. You've everything going to pot, the O5 trying to hold the pieces together.

That doesn't mean anything special in the context of the rest of the piece, at least, not to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean there's my intent, but in poetry it's less important than prose. But do you mean, you don't understand what I was trying to do or you don't understand it, period.

If you want a hint: the last stanza explains everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, I was lost for a second but the last part made me realize what it was. Good work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was a little on the fence at the beginning, but the last stanza nudged me to that +1.

Hooray for more poetry on the mainsite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How many holes before it all unravels…
very nice.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a bit before I realized what it was.

Take your +1, you deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here are the titles of stanzas

Part 1. Television
Part 2. Sleep
Part 3. The Daily Grind
Part 4. Experimentation with the Object
Part 5. The Interview
Part 6. Rumination
Part 7. Special Containment Procedures.

I've been working on this for a long while. Special thanks to Decibelle for remembering the rating module as I scrambled about like a headless chicken fixing things.
